131.title: Ime je znak: names, name days and godparents
131.intro: Names open Croatian culture: half the country celebrates name days, surnames in -ić hide little family histories, and every name has a story. Today's words let you ask about names, explain your own, and understand why your wife's family calendar is full of saints.

items.en: first name and surname
items.note: On every form: ime i prezime. Remember ime declines with the -en- extension: imena, imenu.

items.en: nickname
items.note: G sg. nadimka — the fleeting a drops. Kakav ti je nadimak?

items.en: name day
items.note: The feast day of the saint you are named after — many Croats celebrate it like a second birthday.

items.en: Happy name day!
items.note: Say it to every Ana on 26 July (sveta Ana).

items.en: godfather and godmother
items.note: Also best man / maid of honour at a wedding — kumovi are practically family.

items.en: baptism, christening
items.note: Na krštenju dijete dobiva ime — at the baptism the child receives its name.

items.en: I was named after my grandfather.
items.note: dobiti ime po + locative. A woman says: Dobila sam ime po baki.

items.en: I inherited my grandfather's name.
items.note: naslijediti = to inherit; djedovo — possessive adjective from djed.

items.en: My name means 'victory'.
items.note: značiti = to mean. Get this sentence ready with your own name's meaning.

items.hr: Svi ga zovu Ivo, ali u putovnici piše Ivan.
items.en: Everyone calls him Ivo, but his passport says Ivan.
items.note: Croatian loves short forms: Ivan → Ivo, Katarina → Kata, Josip → Joso.

items.en: Surnames in -ić often mean 'son (of)'.
items.note: Kovačić = the smith's son; Petrić = Petar's son. The -ić is a diminutive.

items.en: Who chose your name?
items.note: izabrati = to choose (pf.). The question that opens every name story.

131.title: Names and customs in practice
131.intro: Lock in the name-day vocabulary and the little grammar around it: po + locative, the ime declension and the fleeting a in nadimak.

questions.prompt: 'I was named after my grandfather.' — Dobio sam ime ______ djedu.
questions.explanation: dobiti ime po + locative: Dobio sam ime po djedu.

questions.prompt: Complete the greeting to Ana on 26 July: Sretan ______!
questions.explanation: Sretan imendan! — Happy name day! The name-day equivalent of Sretan rođendan.

questions.prompt: Pick the genitive: Ne znam podrijetlo tvog ______.
questions.explanation: ime extends its stem outside the nominative: G sg. imena (podrijetlo tvog imena = the origin of your name).

questions.prompt: What does the surname Kovačić literally suggest?
questions.explanation: kovač = smith + the diminutive -ić = the smith's little one / son. Most -ić surnames work this way.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Everyone calls me Ivo.'
questions.explanation: The clitic me sits in second position: Svi me zovu Ivo.

questions.prompt: Complete: On je ______ ime po ocu. (dobiti, past, male)
questions.explanation: dobiti → dobio (m. sg. participle): On je dobio ime po ocu.

questions.prompt: Who are 'kum i kuma'?
questions.explanation: kum and kuma are godparents at a baptism and the witnesses at a wedding — a close, almost family bond.

questions.prompt: Ask about a nickname: Kakav ti je ______?
questions.explanation: nadimak = nickname. Watch the fleeting a in other cases: G sg. nadimka.

132.title: Loanwords that decline: taksi, hobi, intervju, auto
132.intro: Modern Croatian is full of loanwords ending in -i, -u, -o and -e — and they all decline as masculine nouns with tiny, learnable adjustments: nouns in -i insert a -j-, the rest just add the endings. Learn the pattern once and taksi, hobi, intervju and auto behave forever.

items.en: taxi → of / to / by taxi
items.note: Nouns in -i insert -j- before every ending.

items.en: I'm going to the airport by taxi.
items.note: Instrumental of means: taksijem — like vlakom, autobusom.

items.hr: Zvao sam taksi, ali taksija nema.
items.en: I called a taxi, but there's no taxi around.
items.note: nema + genitive: taksija.

items.en: hobby → of a hobby
items.note: Same -j- insertion: hobija, hobiju, hobijem.

items.en: Do you have any hobby?
items.note: Inanimate masculine: A sg. = N sg., so imati hobi.

items.en: I have no time for hobbies.
items.note: Plural hobiji; za + A: za hobije.

items.en: interview → of / in an interview
items.note: Nouns in -u simply add the endings: intervjua, intervjuu, intervjuom.

items.hr: Dao je dugi intervju za radio.
items.en: He gave a long interview for the radio.
items.note: za + A. Radio itself declines tomorrow: radija.

items.en: car → of / to / by car
items.note: auto is masculine although it ends in -o: moj auto, G sg. auta.

items.hr: Auto je na servisu, pa idem tramvajem.
items.en: The car is being serviced, so I'm going by tram.
items.note: na servisu = at the garage; pa = so.

items.en: final (match) → of the final
items.note: Nouns in -e take the endings on the full form: finala, finalu.

items.en: Who is playing in the final?
items.note: u + locative: u finalu.

132.title: Loanwords through the cases
132.intro: Run today's four model nouns through the singular: genitives after nema and bez, instrumentals of transport, locatives of place.

questions.prompt: Complete the genitive: Nema ______ na stanici. (taksi)
questions.explanation: nema + genitive; taksi inserts -j-: taksija.

questions.prompt: 'I'm going to work by taxi.' — Idem na posao ______.
questions.explanation: Instrumental of means with -em after the soft j: taksijem.

questions.prompt: Complete: Nemam ______, samo posao i obitelj. (hobi, genitive)
questions.explanation: nemati + genitive: Nemam hobija — hobi inserts -j- exactly like taksi.

questions.prompt: 'part of the interview' — dio ______
questions.explanation: dio + genitive; intervju just adds -a: dio intervjua.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct form: Vozim se starim ______.
questions.explanation: voziti se + instrumental: starim autom. auto declines like a masculine noun.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'The car is being serviced.'
questions.explanation: na servisu (locative) = at the garage; je in second position.

questions.prompt: Complete: Tko igra u ______? (finale, locative)
questions.explanation: finale adds endings to the full form: u finalu.

questions.prompt: Which of these nouns is masculine despite ending in -o?
questions.explanation: Loanwords like auto (and radio) are masculine: moj auto, taj radio. Selo, pivo and jutro are ordinary neuters.

133.title: radio → radija, Mario → Marija: the -io pattern
133.intro: Nouns and names ending in -io insert a -j- in every case except the nominative: radio → radija, Mario → Marija, studio → studija. It's a tiny rule with constant payoff — Croatian media words and half the men on the Dalmatian coast follow it.

items.en: radio → of / on / with the radio
items.note: The -j- appears everywhere except the nominative.

items.en: I listen to the news on the radio.
items.note: na + locative: na radiju.

items.hr: Čuo sam tu pjesmu na radiju.
items.en: I heard that song on the radio.
items.note: čuti → čuo (m. sg. participle).

items.en: studio → of / in the studio
items.note: Snimamo u studiju. — We're recording in the studio.

items.en: Mario → of / to / with Mario
items.note: Names in -io follow exactly the same rule.

items.hr: Razgovarao sam s Marijem o poslu.
items.en: I talked with Mario about work.
items.note: s + instrumental: s Marijem.

items.en: I'm going to Dario's for coffee.
items.note: kod + genitive: Darija — same pattern as Mario.

items.en: I saw Mario in town.
items.note: Animate masculine: A = G, Marija. Careful — it looks like the female name Marija!

items.hr: Marija sam vidio, a Mariju nisam.
items.en: I saw Mario, but I didn't see Marija.
items.note: Case endings keep them apart: Marija (A of Mario) vs Mariju (A of Marija, the woman).

items.en: Dario works at the radio station.
items.note: raditi na radiju — a fixed phrase.

items.en: That is Mario's car.
items.note: Possessive adjective: Mario → Marijev, with the same -j-.

items.hr: Ne mogu naći radio u autu.
items.en: I can't find the radio in the car.
items.note: Yesterday's auto meets today's radio: u autu, and radio as inanimate A = N.

133.title: The j-insertion in action
133.intro: Decline radio, studio and the names Mario and Dario through real sentences — and learn to tell Marija (of Mario) from Marija (the woman).

questions.prompt: Complete: Slušam vijesti na ______. (radio)
questions.explanation: na + locative with the -j- insertion: na radiju.

questions.prompt: 'with Mario' — Razgovarao sam ______.
questions.explanation: Mario → instrumental Marijem. Careful: s Marijom would mean with Marija, the woman!

questions.prompt: Complete: Idem kod ______ na kavu. (Dario)
questions.explanation: kod + genitive: Dario → Darija, with the inserted -j-.

questions.prompt: Complete: Snimamo pjesmu u ______.
questions.explanation: u + locative: studio → u studiju, never without the -j-.

questions.prompt: 'Vidio sam Marija.' — Whom did I see?
questions.explanation: Animate masculine accusative = genitive: Mario → Marija. Seeing the woman Marija would be: Vidio sam Mariju.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Dario works at the radio station.'
questions.explanation: raditi na radiju — the fixed phrase for working at a radio station.

questions.prompt: Possessive: To je ______ auto. (Mario)
questions.explanation: Masculine possessor takes -ev after the inserted j: Mario → Marijev auto.

questions.prompt: Pick the case after bez: Ne mogu zamisliti jutro bez ______.
questions.explanation: bez + genitive: bez radija — I can't imagine a morning without the radio.

134.title: Kitchen-table verbs: pečem, jedem, mogu
134.intro: Today's verbs live in the kitchen: peći (to bake/roast), jesti (to eat) and their friends. They are consonant-stem verbs with regular -em endings but surprising corners — oni peku, ja mogu — and after today very few present tenses can catch you off guard.

items.en: to bake / roast → I bake…
items.note: Consonant-stem verb. Watch the 3rd plural: oni peku!

items.en: I bake bread every Saturday.
items.note: svaku subotu — accusative of time.

items.en: Grandma is baking cakes for the name day.
items.note: Day 131 meets day 134: za + accusative.

items.en: They are grilling fish on the barbecue.
items.note: 3rd pl. peku — the č becomes k again.

items.en: to eat → I eat…
items.note: The -sti → -dem class; 3rd pl. jedu.

items.en: What do you eat for breakfast?
items.note: za doručak / za ručak / za večeru — for breakfast / lunch / dinner.

items.en: On Sundays we eat together at mum's place.
items.note: nedjeljom — instrumental for a repeated day: 'on Sundays'.

items.en: can → I can…
items.note: 1st sg. and 3rd pl. are both mogu; everything in between has ž.

items.en: I can't eat spicy food.
items.note: Modal + infinitive; ljuto = spicy (also 'angry').

items.en: to sit down → I sit down
items.note: The -sti → -dnem class; perfective, so it mostly appears in commands and futures.

items.en: Sit down, lunch is ready!
items.note: Imperative of sjesti: sjedni! / sjednite!

items.hr: Peče se kruh — cijela kuća miriše.
items.en: Bread is baking — the whole house smells wonderful.
items.note: A se-form sneak preview of day 139's impersonals; mirisati = to smell (nice).

134.title: pečem, jedem, mogu — no surprises left
134.intro: Conjugate the kitchen verbs cold: the tricky third plurals, the mogu/možeš split and the sjesti imperative.

questions.prompt: Complete: Oni ______ ribu na roštilju.
questions.explanation: peći has k in the 3rd plural: oni peku (like moći → oni mogu).

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ______ kruh svaku subotu. (peći)
questions.explanation: peći → pečem in the 1st singular: Pečem kruh.

questions.prompt: Complete: Što ______ za doručak? (jesti, ti)
questions.explanation: jesti → jedem, jedeš: the -sti infinitive hides a d-stem.

questions.prompt: Complete: Djeca ______ juhu.
questions.explanation: 3rd plural of jesti is jedu. Djeca is a collective but takes a plural verb.

questions.prompt: 'I can't come to lunch.' — Ne ______ doći na ručak.
questions.explanation: moći is irregular: ja mogu (never *možem), ti možeš, oni mogu.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Grandma is baking cakes for the name day.'
questions.explanation: Subject–verb–object, then za + accusative: Baka peče kolače za imendan.

questions.prompt: Complete the command: ______, ručak je gotov! (sjesti, vi)
questions.explanation: sjesti → sjednem → imperative sjedni / sjednite.

questions.prompt: Which present form is NOT correct?
questions.explanation: The 3rd plural of peći is peku — *pečeju does not exist.

135.title: dajem, prodajem — and the last irregular noun: dio
135.intro: Verbs in -vati lose the va in the present: davati → dajem, prodavati → prodajem, poznavati → poznajem. And dio (part) — the last irregular noun on your A2 list — swaps -io for -ijel- before every ending: dijela, dijelu, dijelovi. After today, your A2 grammar inventory is complete.

items.en: to give (impf.) → I give…
items.note: -vati → -jem: the va disappears in the present. 3rd pl. daju.

items.en: I give the dog the lunch leftovers.
items.note: Dative psu + accusative ostatke.

items.en: to sell (impf.) → I sell
items.note: Same pattern: prodajem, prodaješ… prodaju.

items.en: The neighbours are selling their flat.
items.note: 3rd pl. prodaju — looks like the noun prodaja, but it's a verb here.

items.en: to know (a person) → I know
items.note: Poznajem ga godinama. — I've known him for years.

items.en: to give / to sell (perfective)
items.note: The perfective partners keep -am: dam, prodam. Prodali smo auto. — We sold the car.

items.en: part → of / to / with a part
items.note: The -io becomes -ijel- before endings, exactly like posao → posla.

items.en: I work most of the day.
items.note: veći dio + genitive — a phrase you can say every single day.

items.en: Divide the cake into eight parts.
items.note: podijeliti na + A; after osam the noun is genitive plural: dijelova.

items.en: This is the most beautiful part of town.
items.note: dio grada, dio filma, dio priče — dio + genitive.

items.en: I'm giving you a piece of the cake.
items.note: Today's verb + today's noun in one sentence.

items.hr: Prodajem bicikl jer mi treba novac.
items.en: I'm selling my bike because I need money.
items.note: treba mi + nominative = I need. More of this pattern on day 139.

135.title: Give, sell, and divide into parts
135.intro: Drill the -vati → -jem present and run dio through its cases — the final pieces of the A2 grammar puzzle.

questions.prompt: Complete: Ja ______ psu hranu. (davati)
questions.explanation: davati → dajem: the -va- drops in the present.

questions.prompt: Complete: Susjedi ______ stan.
questions.explanation: prodavati → prodajem… oni prodaju. *prodavaju does not exist.

questions.prompt: Complete: ______ ga godinama. (poznavati, ja)
questions.explanation: poznavati → poznajem: Poznajem ga godinama = I've known him for years.

questions.prompt: Pick the genitive of dio: Bez tog ______ priča nema smisla.
questions.explanation: bez + genitive; dio → dijela with the -ijel- stem.

questions.prompt: Complete: Podijelili smo kolač na osam ______.
questions.explanation: After osam (5+) the noun takes the genitive plural: osam dijelova.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I work most of the day.'
questions.explanation: veći dio dana = most of the day (dio + genitive), then the verb.

questions.prompt: Perfective past: ______ smo auto prošli tjedan. (prodati, mi, male speakers)
questions.explanation: prodati → prodali (m. pl.): Prodali smo auto — one completed sale, so perfective.

questions.prompt: Which pair is matched correctly (infinitive → present)?
questions.explanation: All -vati verbs switch to -jem: dajem, prodajem, poznajem. The -vam forms don't exist.

136.title: The story of your name: ready-made frames
136.intro: Output day. These twelve frames build your 90-second talk: where your name comes from, who chose it, your nicknames and how Croatians handle it. Swap in your own details, say each line aloud, then assemble the story.

items.hr: Moje ime dolazi iz latinskog jezika.
items.en: My name comes from Latin.
items.note: iz + genitive: iz latinskog, iz grčkog, iz hebrejskog, iz španjolskog.

items.en: My mum chose my name.
items.note: Clitic order: Ime mi je izabrala… — dative mi before the auxiliary je.

items.hr: Dobio sam ime po djedu, koji se zvao isto.
items.en: I was named after my grandfather, who had the same name.
items.note: koji se zvao isto — a relative clause you can reuse anywhere.

items.en: My name means 'strong and brave'.
items.note: Look up your name's real meaning and slot it in.

items.en: At home they call me by a short form.
items.note: skraćeno = in shortened form; zvati koga + nickname.

items.hr: Hrvati moje ime izgovaraju malo drukčije.
items.en: Croatians pronounce my name a bit differently.
items.note: izgovarati = to pronounce; drukčije = differently.

items.hr: Na hrvatskom bi moje ime glasilo…
items.en: In Croatian my name would be…
items.note: glasiti = to read / sound (of names and texts); conditional bi glasilo.

items.en: My name is hard to decline.
items.note: sklanjati se = to decline (grammar). Ask your wife: Kako se moje ime sklanja?

items.hr: U mojoj obitelji svi nasljeđuju imena.
items.en: In my family everyone inherits names.
items.note: nasljeđivati (impf.) → nasljeđujem — the process, not one event.

items.hr: Da imam sina, dao bih mu ime po ocu.
items.en: If I had a son, I would name him after my father.
items.note: Unreal condition: da + present, then the conditional dao bih.

items.hr: Nisam znao da je to ime tako često u Hrvatskoj.
items.en: I didn't know that name was so common in Croatia.
items.note: često = frequent/common; the je sits right after da.

items.hr: A ti? Po kome si ti dobila ime?
items.en: And you? Who were you named after?
items.note: po kome = after whom. End your talk by turning it into a conversation.

136.title: Assemble the name story
136.intro: Test the frames before you deliver the talk: clitic order, po + locative, the conditional and the connectors that hold a story together.

questions.prompt: Complete the clitic: Ime ______ je izabrala mama. ('to me')
questions.explanation: The dative clitic mi comes before the auxiliary je: Ime mi je izabrala mama.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence: 'I was named after my grandfather.'
questions.explanation: Named after = dobiti ime po + locative: po djedu, po baki, po ocu.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'My name comes from Latin.'
questions.explanation: iz + genitive of the language adjective: iz latinskog (jezika).

questions.prompt: Complete: Moje ime ______ 'kralj'. (to mean)
questions.explanation: značiti = to mean: Moje ime znači… — the heart of your talk.

questions.prompt: Pick the conditional: Da imam sina, ______ mu ime po ocu.
questions.explanation: Unreal condition: da + present in the if-clause, conditional (dao bih) in the main clause.

questions.prompt: What does 'po kome' mean in 'Po kome si dobila ime?'
questions.explanation: po + locative of tko (kome): named after whom.

questions.prompt: Complete: Hrvati moje ime ______ malo drukčije. (izgovarati, oni)
questions.explanation: izgovarati is a regular -am verb: oni izgovaraju.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'I didn't know that name was so common.'
questions.answer: Nisam znao da je to ime tako često
questions.explanation: Nisam can stand first; in the da-clause the clitic je comes immediately after da.

136.title: Proba: priča o mom imenu
136.intro: You rehearse your 90-second name talk with your wife as the audience — and the clock. Read your lines ('Me') aloud; every one of them is a frame from today's list with your details slotted in.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Hajde, vježbajmo. Ispričaj mi priču o svom imenu — imaš devedeset sekundi!
lines.en: Come on, let's practise. Tell me the story of your name — you have ninety seconds!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Može. Moje ime dolazi iz latinskog jezika i znači 'snažan'.
lines.en: Okay. My name comes from Latin and means 'strong'.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Lijep početak. Tko ti je izabrao ime?
lines.en: A nice start. Who chose your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Ime mi je izabrala mama. Dobio sam ga po djedu, koji se zvao isto.
lines.en: My mum chose my name. I got it after my grandfather, who had the same name.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A imaš li nadimak?
lines.en: And do you have a nickname?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Imam. Kod kuće me zovu skraćeno, ali službeno se zovem punim imenom.
lines.en: I do. At home they call me by a short form, but officially I go by my full name.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Kako Hrvati izgovaraju tvoje ime?
lines.en: How do Croatians pronounce your name?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Malo drukčije nego kod nas. I nisam znao da se moje ime tako teško sklanja!
lines.en: A bit differently than back home. And I didn't know my name was so hard to decline!

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! Navikni se. A da imamo sina, koje bi mu ime dao?
lines.en: Ha! Get used to it. And if we had a son, what name would you give him?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Dao bih mu ime po tvom ocu. Tako bi naslijedio i ime i priču.
lines.en: I would name him after your father. That way he would inherit both a name and a story.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: To je jako lijepo. Vidiš, priča je gotova — i bila je duža od devedeset sekundi!
lines.en: That's really lovely. See, the story is done — and it was longer than ninety seconds!

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Onda idem ispočetka. Vježba čini majstora!
lines.en: Then I'll start over. Practice makes perfect!

137.title: Questions that open family stories
137.intro: Speaking day. Tonight you interview your wife about the names in her family — and these questions carry the whole conversation. They recycle po + locative, possessives, svoj and the perfekt, but they feel like curiosity, not grammar. Say each one aloud before you use it live.

items.en: Who were you named after? (to a woman)
items.note: To a man: Po kome si dobio ime?

items.en: Do you celebrate your name day? How?
items.note: slaviti = to celebrate; li right after the verb makes the question.

items.en: Where does your surname come from?
items.note: potjecati → potječe = to originate, stem from.

items.hr: Kako su se zvali tvoji baka i djed?
items.en: What were your grandma and grandpa called?
items.note: zvati se in the perfekt: su se zvali.

items.en: Who were your godparents?
items.note: Dative ti = 'to you'; kumovi — day 131's word in the plural.

items.hr: Postoji li neka priča o tvom imenu?
items.en: Is there a story behind your name?
items.note: postojati → postoji = to exist; priča o + locative.

items.hr: Je li to ime često u tvojoj obitelji?
items.en: Is that name common in your family?
items.note: Je li…? — the standard yes/no question with je.

items.hr: Koje je najstarije ime u obitelji?
items.en: What is the oldest name in the family?
items.note: najstarije — superlative, neuter to agree with ime.

items.hr: Ispričaj mi nešto o svojoj baki.
items.en: Tell me something about your grandma.
items.note: svojoj — her own grandma, because she is the subject; ispričati = to tell (a story).

items.en: Really? I didn't know that!
items.note: Your best listening reaction — use it often tonight.

items.hr: Čekaj, ponovi — tko je kome dao ime?
items.en: Wait, say that again — who gave whom the name?
items.note: Clarify without switching to English: ponovi = repeat (imperative).

items.hr: Kod nas nema imendana — to mi je novo.
items.en: Where I'm from there are no name days — that's new to me.
items.note: nema + genitive; to mi je novo = that's new to me.

137.title: Warm-up: interview your wife
137.intro: Two listening passes and six quick checks — prime your ears and your questions before the real conversation about names and family history.

questions.prompt: Listen to your wife's answer: when and where does she celebrate her name day?
options: In July, at her mum's place
questions.answer: In July, at her mum's place
questions.explanation: Moj imendan je u srpnju… slavimo ga kod moje mame — her name day is in July and the whole family comes to her mum's for lunch.
questions.audioText: Moj imendan je u srpnju, na blagdan svete Ane. Uvijek ga slavimo kod moje mame — dođe cijela obitelj na ručak i mama peče kolače.

questions.prompt: Listen to her talk about her family: what does her grandma's surname mean?
questions.explanation: Prezime moje bake bilo je Kovačić — to znači 'kovačev sin': her great-grandfather was a blacksmith (kovač).
questions.audioText: Moje prezime potječe iz Dalmacije. Prezime moje bake bilo je Kovačić — to znači 'kovačev sin', jer je njezin pradjed bio kovač.

questions.prompt: Complete the question: Odakle ______ tvoje prezime? (potjecati)
questions.explanation: potjecati → potječe: Odakle potječe tvoje prezime? — Where does your surname come from?

questions.prompt: You're asking your wife. Pick the correct form: 'Who were you named after?'
questions.explanation: To a woman the participle is feminine (dobila), and 'named after' is po + locative (po kome).

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Tell me something about your grandma.'
questions.answer: Ispričaj mi nešto o svojoj baki
questions.explanation: Imperative first, clitic mi second, then o + locative with svoj: o svojoj baki.

questions.prompt: React to a surprise: Stvarno? To nisam ______! (znati, male speaker)
questions.explanation: znati → znao (m. sg.): To nisam znao! — I didn't know that!

questions.prompt: 'Kod nas nema imendana.' — Which case does nema take?
questions.explanation: nema + genitive for absence: nema imendana, nema kruha, nema problema.

questions.prompt: Ask for a repeat: Čekaj, ______ — nisam razumio. (repeat, imperative to ti)
questions.explanation: ponoviti → ponovi! — your clarification tool that keeps the conversation in Croatian.

137.title: Večernji razgovor: imena u tvojoj obitelji
137.intro: Tonight you hold the microphone: you interview your wife about the names in her family. Read your lines ('Me') aloud — they are exactly today's questions and reactions, deployed live.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Večeras te ja intervjuiram. Po kome si dobila ime?
lines.en: Tonight I'm interviewing you. Who were you named after?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Po baki! Ona se zvala isto, a i njezina mama.
lines.en: After my grandma! She had the same name, and so did her mum.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Stvarno? To nisam znao. Znači, tri generacije nose isto ime?
lines.en: Really? I didn't know that. So three generations carry the same name?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Da, kod nas se imena nasljeđuju. A slavimo i imendan, naravno.
lines.en: Yes, in our family names are inherited. And we celebrate the name day too, of course.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kad ti je imendan? Kod nas toga nema, pa uvijek zaboravim.
lines.en: When is your name day? We don't have that back home, so I always forget.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: U srpnju. Mama uvijek peče kolače i dođe cijela obitelj.
lines.en: In July. Mum always bakes cakes and the whole family comes.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: A odakle potječe tvoje prezime? Zvuči jako staro.
lines.en: And where does your surname come from? It sounds very old.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Iz Dalmacije. Znači 'kovačev sin' — pradjed je bio kovač.
lines.en: From Dalmatia. It means 'the smith's son' — my great-grandfather was a blacksmith.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Čekaj, ponovi — tko je bio kovač? Tvoj pradjed?
lines.en: Wait, say that again — who was a blacksmith? Your great-grandfather?

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Da, pradjed. Imao je malu radionicu pokraj mora.
lines.en: Yes, my great-grandfather. He had a small workshop by the sea.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Krasna priča. U tvojoj obitelji svako ime ima svoju priču.
lines.en: A wonderful story. In your family every name has its own story.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Zato i kažemo: ime je znak. A sad ti — ispričaj mi nešto o svom djedu!
lines.en: That's why we say: a name is a sign. And now you — tell me something about your grandfather!

138.title: Hrvati u svijetu: the diaspora vocabulary
138.intro: More Croats and their descendants live abroad than in Croatia — which is why iseljeništvo, domovina and korijeni sit behind half of Croatian family stories. Today's words let you tell a migration story: who left, who arrived, why, and what they kept.

items.en: emigration; the diaspora (as a whole)
items.note: hrvatsko iseljeništvo = Croats abroad, collectively.

items.en: diaspora
items.note: The international word works too: živjeti u dijaspori.

items.en: homeland
items.note: stara domovina = the old country — how emigrants call Croatia.

items.en: emigrant / immigrant
items.note: iz- = out (leaving), u- = in (arriving) — the prefixes show the direction.

items.en: border
items.note: prijeći granicu = to cross the border.

items.en: citizenship
items.note: imati dvojno državljanstvo = to have dual citizenship — very common in Croatian families.

items.en: custom, tradition
items.note: čuvati stare običaje = to keep old customs.

items.en: roots
items.note: Tražim svoje korijene. — I'm searching for my roots.

items.en: generation
items.note: druga generacija iseljenika = second-generation emigrants.

items.en: to move (change residence)
items.note: Preselili smo se u Zagreb prije dvije godine.

items.hr: Moja se obitelj odselila zbog posla.
items.en: My family moved away because of work.
items.note: odseliti se = to move away; zbog + genitive = because of.

items.hr: Vratili su se u staru domovinu.
items.en: They returned to the old homeland.
items.note: vratiti se u + accusative — motion back.

items.hr: Hrvatske zajednice žive u Njemačkoj, Americi, Australiji i Čileu.
items.en: Croatian communities live in Germany, America, Australia and Chile.
items.note: zajednica = community; u + locative for countries: u Njemačkoj, u Americi.

138.title: Tell a migration story
138.intro: Check the direction prefixes (iz- vs u-), the cases behind zbog and u, and the phrases that carry every family's migration story.

questions.prompt: Someone who has just arrived to live in a new country is a(n) ______.
questions.explanation: u- = in (arriving): useljenik = immigrant. iz- = out (leaving): iseljenik = emigrant.

questions.prompt: Complete: Imam dvojno ______. (citizenship)
questions.explanation: dvojno državljanstvo = dual citizenship — a word every diaspora family knows.

questions.prompt: Complete the auxiliary: Preselili ______ se u Zagreb prije dvije godine.
questions.explanation: Perfekt of preseliti se: Preselili smo se… — smo before se in the clitic chain.

questions.prompt: 'They moved away because of work.' — Odselili su se ______ posla.
questions.explanation: zbog + genitive = because of: zbog posla, zbog rata, zbog ljubavi.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'They returned to the old homeland.'
questions.answer: Vratili su se u staru domovinu
questions.explanation: Clitic order su + se in second position, then u + accusative of direction: u staru domovinu.

questions.prompt: Croatian communities live in Germany: Žive u ______.
questions.explanation: Living in a country = u + locative: u Njemačkoj. (u Njemačku would be motion: to Germany.)

questions.prompt: Complete: Tražim svoje ______. (roots)
questions.explanation: korijeni (roots) in the accusative plural: tražim svoje korijene.

questions.prompt: What does 'čuvati stare običaje' mean?
questions.explanation: čuvati = to keep/guard; običaj = custom. Diaspora families čuvaju stare običaje.

139.title: Kaže se: the impersonal toolkit
139.intro: Croatian talks about 'people in general' and inner states without a subject: Kaže se… (one says), Ovdje se ne puši (no smoking), Hladno mi je (I'm cold). These patterns are indispensable and utterly unlike English — collect them today as fixed templates.

items.hr: Kako se kaže 'name day' na hrvatskom?
items.en: How do you say 'name day' in Croatian?
items.note: The most useful sentence in this whole app: se + 3rd singular.

items.en: You say 'imendan'.
items.note: The answering pattern — no subject at all.

items.en: No smoking here.
items.note: se-impersonal: literally 'here it is not smoked'.

items.hr: U Hrvatskoj se pije puno kave.
items.en: In Croatia a lot of coffee is drunk.
items.note: The 'people in general' pattern — no one in particular drinks it, everyone does.

items.en: The food is good here. (One eats well here.)
items.note: Day 134's jesti inside an impersonal.

items.en: You can pay by card.
items.note: može se + infinitive = one can; karticom — instrumental of means.

items.hr: Ne smije se parkirati na pločniku.
items.en: You must not park on the pavement.
items.note: ne smije se = it is not allowed — the language of signs.

items.en: One needs to practise a lot.
items.note: treba + infinitive, no subject: general necessity.

items.en: I am cold.
items.note: State + dative experiencer. Never *Ja sam hladan — that means you're a cold person!

items.en: She is bored.
items.note: joj = to her. Vruće mu je — he is hot.

items.hr: Žao nam je što ne možete doći.
items.en: We are sorry that you can't come.
items.note: žao + dative + je; što introduces the reason.

items.hr: Bilo je vruće, a sad je ugodno.
items.en: It was hot, and now it's pleasant.
items.note: Past of impersonals: neuter singular — bilo je.

items.en: One should have studied more!
items.note: Past of treba: trebalo je — also neuter singular.

139.title: No subject, no problem
139.intro: Build se-impersonals and dative-state sentences until they come out automatically — including their neuter past.

questions.prompt: Complete: Kako se ______ 'homeland' na hrvatskom?
questions.explanation: Kako se kaže…? = How do you say…? — the impersonal question you'll use forever. (Answer: domovina.)

questions.prompt: Pick the correct sign: 'No smoking here.'
questions.explanation: se-impersonal in the 3rd singular, with se in second position: Ovdje se ne puši.

questions.prompt: Pick the correct sentence: 'I am cold.'
questions.explanation: Neuter adjective + dative experiencer: Hladno mi je. Ja sam hladan would describe your personality.

questions.prompt: Complete: Dosadno ______ je. (to her)
questions.explanation: Dative clitic joj = to her: Dosadno joj je — she is bored.

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'In Croatia a lot of coffee is drunk.'
questions.answer: U Hrvatskoj se pije puno kave
questions.explanation: se sits in second position after the opening phrase U Hrvatskoj; puno + genitive (kave).

questions.prompt: Put 'Vruće je.' into the past.
questions.explanation: Impersonal past always takes the neuter singular participle: Bilo je vruće.

questions.prompt: Complete: Može se ______ karticom. (platiti)
questions.explanation: može se + infinitive: Može se platiti karticom — you can pay by card.

questions.prompt: Complete: Žao ______ je što ne možeš doći. (to us)
questions.explanation: žao + dative: Žao nam je — we are sorry.

questions.prompt: What does 'Trebalo je više učiti!' mean?
questions.explanation: Past of impersonal treba is the neuter trebalo je: Trebalo je više učiti — the classic post-exam sigh.

140.title: Comparing two worlds: frames that stay friendly
140.intro: Every foreigner in Croatia gets asked to compare: food, work, coffee, family. Today's frames recycle the whole phase — comparatives, impersonals, dative experiencers — and end with the diplomatic lines that keep the comparison warm: nije bolje ni gore, samo je drukčije.

items.hr: U Hrvatskoj se večera kasnije nego kod nas.
items.en: In Croatia dinner is eaten later than back home.
items.note: Impersonal + comparative adverb — the day's whole formula in one line.

items.hr: Kod nas se radi duže, ali se manje živi.
items.en: Back home people work longer, but live less.
items.note: kod nas = where I'm from; duže and manje — comparative adverbs.

items.hr: Kava se ovdje pije polako, uz razgovor.
items.en: Coffee here is drunk slowly, over conversation.
items.note: uz + accusative = accompanied by: uz razgovor, uz kolač.

items.en: Life here is more relaxed.
items.note: opušten → opušteniji — regular -iji comparative.

items.en: The food is fresher and tastier.
items.note: svjež → svježiji, ukusan → ukusniji (fleeting a drops).

items.hr: Plaće su manje, ali je život jeftiniji.
items.en: Salaries are smaller, but life is cheaper.
items.note: mali → manji (irregular); jeftin → jeftiniji.

items.hr: Obitelj je ovdje važnija nego karijera.
items.en: Family here is more important than career.
items.note: nego + nominative when comparing two nouns.

items.en: What I like most is the sea.
items.note: sviđa mi se + nominative — the dative experiencer from day 139.

items.en: The hardest thing for me is learning the cases.
items.note: najteže + dative + infinitive — an honest, charming answer.

items.hr: I jedno i drugo ima svoje prednosti.
items.en: Both have their advantages.
items.note: prednost (f., i-declension) = advantage. The diplomatic answer.

items.hr: Nije bolje ni gore — samo je drukčije.
items.en: It's not better or worse — just different.
items.note: Your culture-comparison lifeline: bolje/gore are the irregular adverb comparatives.

items.hr: Što ti se najviše sviđa u Hrvatskoj?
items.en: What do you like most about Croatia?
items.note: The classic question every foreigner gets daily — have your answer ready, cold.

140.title: Compare without offending
140.intro: Comparatives, impersonals and the dative experiencer — the grammar of the conversation you'll have at every Croatian table.

questions.prompt: Complete: U Hrvatskoj se večera kasnije ______ kod nas.
questions.explanation: Before a phrase like kod nas, comparison uses nego: kasnije nego kod nas.

questions.prompt: Complete the comparative: Život je ovdje ______. (opušten)
questions.explanation: Regular -iji comparative: opušten → opušteniji — more relaxed.

questions.prompt: Pick the comparative of jeftin:
questions.explanation: jeftin takes the regular -iji: jeftiniji; superlative najjeftiniji.

questions.prompt: 'Najviše mi se sviđa more.' — Who does the liking?
options: I do — mi is the dative experiencer
questions.answer: I do — mi is the dative experiencer
questions.explanation: sviđati se puts the liker in the dative (mi) and what is liked in the nominative (more).

questions.prompt: Put the words in order: 'Coffee here is drunk slowly.'
questions.explanation: The clitic se takes second position right after Kava: Kava se ovdje pije polako.

questions.prompt: Complete: Plaće su ______, ali je život jeftiniji. (mali, comparative)
questions.explanation: mali has the irregular comparative manji; feminine plural: plaće su manje.

questions.prompt: Pick the diplomatic answer: 'It's not better or worse — just different.'
options: Nije bolje ni gore — samo je drukčije.
Nije dobro ni loše — sve je isto.
Bolje je i gore u isto vrijeme.
questions.answer: Nije bolje ni gore — samo je drukčije.
questions.explanation: bolje/gore are the irregular adverb comparatives of dobro/loše; drukčije = different(ly).

questions.prompt: The classic question: Što ti se najviše ______ u Hrvatskoj?
questions.explanation: Što ti se najviše sviđa u Hrvatskoj? — have your answer ready before dinner tonight.

140.title: Dva svijeta za jednim stolom
140.intro: Over dinner, your wife asks the classic question — and you compare your two worlds using today's frames: impersonals, comparatives and the diplomatic finish. Read your lines ('Me') aloud.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Reci mi iskreno — što ti se najviše sviđa u Hrvatskoj?
lines.en: Tell me honestly — what do you like most about Croatia?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Najviše mi se sviđa more. I kava — ovdje se kava pije polako, uz razgovor.
lines.en: What I like most is the sea. And coffee — here coffee is drunk slowly, over conversation.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: A kod vas? Kako se pije kava u tvojoj zemlji?
lines.en: And back home? How is coffee drunk in your country?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kod nas se sve radi brže. Ljudi kupe kavu i piju je u autu.
lines.en: Back home everything is done faster. People buy coffee and drink it in the car.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: To mi zvuči stresno. A posao?
lines.en: That sounds stressful to me. And work?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Kod nas se radi duže, ali mislim da je život ovdje opušteniji.
lines.en: Back home people work longer, but I think life here is more relaxed.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Plaće su ovdje manje, znaš.
lines.en: Salaries are smaller here, you know.

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Istina, ali je život jeftiniji, a obitelj je važnija nego karijera.
lines.en: True, but life is cheaper, and family is more important than career.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Postoji li nešto što ti je ovdje teško?
lines.en: Is there anything that's hard for you here?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Najteže mi je naučiti padeže! I večera se jede prekasno za mene.
lines.en: The hardest thing for me is learning the cases! And dinner is eaten too late for me.

lines.speaker: Partner
lines.hr: Ha! Navikni se. Pa što je na kraju bolje — tvoja zemlja ili moja?
lines.en: Ha! Get used to it. So which is better in the end — your country or mine?

lines.speaker: Me
lines.hr: Nije bolje ni gore — samo je drukčije. I jedno i drugo ima svoje prednosti.
lines.en: It's not better or worse — just different. Both have their advantages.
